title: Apply: Modern Debates over Elections and Voting Rights
description: Apply your knowledge about the modern debates over elections and voting rights in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Elections and Voting Rights: From the Founding through the Civil Rights Movement
Modern Debates over Elections and Voting Rights: Part II
Apply: Modern Debates over Elections and Voting Rights

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s majority opinion in *Citizens United v. FEC* (2010).**

For these reasons, political speech must [[☃ definition 1]] against laws that would suppress it, whether by design or [[☃ definition 2]]. Laws that burden political speech are “subject to strict scrutiny,” which requires the Government to prove that the restriction “furthers a compelling interest and is narrowly tailored to achieve that interest.” . . . [T]he Government may not suppress political speech on the basis of the speaker’s corporate identity. No sufficient governmental interest justifies limits on the political speech of nonprofit or for-profit corporations.

**Which statement best summarizes the Court’s decision in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: win out; be stronger in influence than

choices.content: Laws that limit political speech must meet strict criteria to be constitutional.
Laws that regulate political speech are not subject to any scrutiny or requirements.
Limits to political speech by nonprofit or for-profit corporations are lawful.
Political speech is less protected if it comes from a corporate speaker.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s majority opinion in *Citizens United v. FEC* (2010).**

For these reasons, political speech must [[☃ definition 1]] against laws that would suppress it, whether by design or [[☃ definition 2]]. Laws that burden political speech are “subject to strict scrutiny,” which requires the Government to prove that the restriction “furthers a compelling interest and is narrowly tailored to achieve that interest.” . . . [T]he Government may not suppress political speech on the basis of the speaker’s corporate identity. No sufficient governmental interest justifies limits on the political speech of nonprofit or for-profit corporations.

**What was the impact of the decision in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: win out; be stronger in influence than

choices.content: Any limits on direct donations to political campaigns are unconstitutional.
Limits on direct political donations must be ideologically neutral.
Corporations cannot spend money on political advertising, as they are not individuals.
Corporations can spend unlimited funds on advertising for campaigns.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s majority opinion in *Reynolds v. Sims* (1964).**

Legislators represent people, not trees or acres. Legislators are elected by voters, not farms or cities or economic interests. As long as ours is a representative form of government, and our legislatures are those instruments of government elected directly by and directly representative of the people, the right to elect legislators in a free and [[☃ definition 1]] fashion is a bedrock of our political system. . . .

The Equal Protection Clause demands no less than substantially equal state legislative representation for all citizens, of all places as well as of all races.

**Which statement best summarizes the Court’s decision in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Legislative districts should be based on physical size, not on the number of people who live in a place.
To ensure that every person is represented equally, legislative districts must be drawn based on population.
Grouping people based on their political party is a form of unacceptable gerrymandering that violates the Equal Protection Clause.
The right to vote is an inalienable right that governments cannot and should not take away from citizens.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s majority opinion in *Reynolds v. Sims* (1964).**

Legislators represent people, not trees or acres. Legislators are elected by voters, not farms or cities or economic interests. As long as ours is a representative form of government, and our legislatures are those instruments of government elected directly by and directly representative of the people, the right to elect legislators in a free and [[☃ definition 1]] fashion is a bedrock of our political system. . . .

The Equal Protection Clause demands no less than substantially equal state legislative representation for all citizens, of all places as well as of all races.

**What was the impact of the decision in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It prohibited states from racial gerrymandering.
It defined the responsibilities of state legislators.
It established the “one person, one vote” principle.
It upheld partisan gerrymandering as constitutional.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s majority opinion in *Shaw v. Reno* (1993).**

[W]e believe that [[☃ definition 1]] is one area in which appearances do matter. A reapportionment plan that includes in one district individuals who belong to the same race, but who are otherwise widely separated by geographical and political boundaries, and who may have little in common with one another but the color of their skin, bears an uncomfortable resemblance to political [[☃ definition 2]]. It reinforces the perception that members of the same racial group—regardless of their age, education, economic status, or the community in which they live—think alike, share the same political interests, and will prefer the same candidates at the polls. We have rejected such perceptions elsewhere as impermissible racial stereotypes. . . . By [[☃ definition 3]] such notions, a racial gerrymander may [[☃ definition 4]] the very patterns of racial bloc voting that majority-minority districting is sometimes said to counteract. . . .

**Which statement best summarizes the Court’s decision in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: drawing congressional districts in a new way based on changes in the population
a formal policy of segregation based on race

choices.content: Reapportionment should avoid creating districts based only on race, as this can reinforce harmful stereotypes and lead to unfair voting patterns.
Because historical segregation created a kind of apartheid in the U.S., states must take race into account when creating congressional districts.
Any form of gerrymandering, including partisan and racial gerrymandering, is wrong and violates the Equal Protection Clause.
States have the power to form congressional districts as they see fit, and the federal government cannot regulate those decisions in any way.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s majority opinion in *Shaw v. Reno* (1993).**

[W]e believe that [[☃ definition 1]] is one area in which appearances do matter. A reapportionment plan that includes in one district individuals who belong to the same race, but who are otherwise widely separated by geographical and political boundaries, and who may have little in common with one another but the color of their skin, bears an uncomfortable resemblance to political [[☃ definition 2]]. It reinforces the perception that members of the same racial group—regardless of their age, education, economic status, or the community in which they live—think alike, share the same political interests, and will prefer the same candidates at the polls. We have rejected such perceptions elsewhere as impermissible racial stereotypes. . . . By [[☃ definition 3]] such notions, a racial gerrymander may [[☃ definition 4]] the very patterns of racial bloc voting that majority-minority districting is sometimes said to counteract. . . .

**What was the impact of the decision in *Shaw v. Reno*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: drawing congressional districts in a new way based on changes in the population
a formal policy of segregation based on race

choices.content: Reapportionment is always unconstitutional, so districts may not be redrawn.
Partisan gerrymandering is constitutional so long as race is not a factor.
The use of race in redistricting must be subject to strict scrutiny.
Partisan gerrymandering is a political question the Court cannot rule on.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from President Dwight Eisenhower’s 1954 State of the Union address.**

For years our citizens between the ages of eighteen and twenty-one have, in time of peril, been summoned to fight for America. They should participate in the political process that produces this fateful summons. I urge Congress to propose to the states a constitutional amendment permitting citizens to vote when they reach the age of eighteen.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The minimum voting age should be lowered to eighteen during wartime, but return to age twenty-one during peacetime.
The voting age should be lowered to eighteen, as adults under twenty-one can be called to defend the United States during wars.
The voting age should increase to twenty-one to best represent the interests of those enlisted in the military.
The minimum voting age should remain at twenty-one, but adults who are under twenty-one should not be allowed to serve in the military.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from President Dwight Eisenhower’s 1954 State of the Union address.**

For years our citizens between the ages of eighteen and twenty-one have, in time of peril, been summoned to fight for America. They should participate in the political process that produces this fateful summons. I urge Congress to propose to the states a constitutional amendment permitting citizens to vote when they reach the age of eighteen.

**Which amendment to the Constitution accomplished the change that President Eisenhower argues for in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Fifteenth Amendment
The Nineteenth Amendment
The Twenty-Fourth Amendment
The Twenty-Sixth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the  Supreme Court’s majority opinion  in *Oregon v. Mitchell* (1970).**

The Voting Rights Act Amendments of 1970, now before this Court, [[☃ definition 1]] dissatisfaction of Congress with the voting age set by many of the states for national elections. . . . Congress has ultimate supervisory power over congressional elections. Similarly, it is the [[☃ definition 2]] of Congress to oversee the conduct of presidential and vice-presidential elections and to set the qualifications for voters for electors for those offices. . . .

On the other hand, the Constitution was also intended to preserve to the states the power that even the colonies had to establish and maintain their own separate and independent governments, except insofar as the Constitution itself commands otherwise. . . . The framers of the Constitution intended the states to keep for themselves, as provided in the Tenth Amendment, the power to regulate elections.. . . No function is more essential to the separate and independent existence of the states and their governments than the power to determine, within the limits of the Constitution, the qualifications of their own voters for state, county, and municipal offices and the nature of their own machinery for filling local public offices. . . .

In this case, it is the judgment of the Court that [the law] lowering the voting age to eighteen is invalid as applied to voters in state and local elections. It is also the judgment of the Court that [the law] is valid with respect to national elections.

**Which statement best summarizes the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Congress has the power to recommend voter qualifications for federal elections, but the executive of each state must approve any recommendation.
Congress has no power to set voter qualifications for elections because states retain the sole power to manage elections and set qualifications.
Congress has the power to set voter qualifications for federal elections, but states have the authority to determine qualifications for state and local elections.
Congress can override state voter qualifications in all levels of elections if they find that the qualifications discriminate against a particular group.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the  Supreme Court’s majority opinion  in *Oregon v. Mitchell* (1970).**

The Voting Rights Act Amendments of 1970, now before this Court, [[☃ definition 1]] dissatisfaction of Congress with the voting age set by many of the states for national elections. . . . Congress has ultimate supervisory power over congressional elections. Similarly, it is the [[☃ definition 2]] of Congress to oversee the conduct of presidential and vice-presidential elections and to set the qualifications for voters for electors for those offices. . . .

On the other hand, the Constitution was also intended to preserve to the states the power that even the colonies had to establish and maintain their own separate and independent governments, except insofar as the Constitution itself commands otherwise. . . . The framers of the Constitution intended the states to keep for themselves, as provided in the Tenth Amendment, the power to regulate elections.. . . No function is more essential to the separate and independent existence of the states and their governments than the power to determine, within the limits of the Constitution, the qualifications of their own voters for state, county, and municipal offices and the nature of their own machinery for filling local public offices. . . .

In this case, it is the judgment of the Court that [the law] lowering the voting age to eighteen is invalid as applied to voters in state and local elections. It is also the judgment of the Court that [the law] is valid with respect to national elections.

**Which action did Congress take to override this decision?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Ratify the Twenty-Sixth Amendment to lower the national voting age to eighteen years old
Pass the National Voter Registration Act to automatically register voters as soon as they reached adulthood
Ratify the Twenty-Seventh Amendment to give Congress the power to set voter qualifications
Pass the Help America Vote Act to set new voter qualifications in Congressional elections

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s majority opinion in *Crawford v. Marion County Election Board* (2008), in which the Court ruled on whether requiring photo identification to vote violated a person’s voting rights.**

The fact that most voters already possess a valid driver’s license, or some other form of acceptable identification, would not save the statute under our reasoning in *Harper,* if the State required voters to pay a tax or a fee to obtain a new photo identification. But just as other States provide free voter registration cards, the photo identification cards issued by Indiana’s Bureau of Motor Vehicles (BMV) are also free. For most voters who need them, the inconvenience of making a trip to the BMV, gathering the required documents, and posing for a photograph surely does not qualify as a substantial burden on the right to vote, or even represent a significant increase over the usual burdens of voting.

**Which statement best summarizes the Supreme Court’s opinion in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Voter ID requirements are constitutional, as people need a driver’s license to operate a motor vehicle.
Voter ID requirements are constitutional, as they do not unnecessarily burden potential voters.
Voter ID requirements are unconstitutional, as they require voters to pay for identification to vote, acting like a poll tax.
Voter ID requirements are unconstitutional, as the time it takes to obtain identification is an example of an unnecessary burden.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s majority opinion in *Crawford v. Marion County Election Board* (2008), in which the Court ruled on whether requiring photo identification to vote violated a person’s voting rights.**

The fact that most voters already possess a valid driver’s license, or some other form of acceptable identification, would not save the statute under our reasoning in *Harper,* if the State required voters to pay a tax or a fee to obtain a new photo identification. But just as other States provide free voter registration cards, the photo identification cards issued by Indiana’s Bureau of Motor Vehicles (BMV) are also free. For most voters who need them, the inconvenience of making a trip to the BMV, gathering the required documents, and posing for a photograph surely does not qualify as a substantial burden on the right to vote, or even represent a significant increase over the usual burdens of voting.

**According to the Court, what condition would Voter ID laws need to meet to be unconstitutional?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To require that voters have state-issued identification
To permit multiple forms of identification
To accept only photo identification
To require a significant financial burden
